title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Five: Which is far too full of washing (Fragment 2)
text:
Howl came out of the bathroom just then in a waft of steamy perfume. He looked marvelously spruce. Even the silver inlets and embroidery on his suit seemed to have become brighter. He took one look and backed into the bathroom again with a blue-and-silver sleeve protecting his head.

“Stop it, woman!” he said. “Leave those poor spiders alone!”

“These cobwebs are a disgrace!” Sophie declared, fetching them down in bundles.

“Then get them down and leave the spiders,” said Howl.

Probably he had a wicked affinity with spiders, Sophie thought. “They’ll only make more webs,” she said.

“And kill flies, which is very useful,” said Howl. “Keep that broom still while I cross my own room, please.”

Sophie leaned on the broom and watched Howl cross the room and pick up his guitar. As he put his hand on the door latch, she said, “If the red blob leads to Kingsbury and the blue blob goes to Porthaven, where does the black blob take you?”

“What a nosy old woman you are!” said Howl. “That leads to my private bolt hole and you are not being told where it is.” He opened the door onto the wide, moving moorland and the hills.

“When will you be back, Howl?” Michael asked a little despairingly.

Howl pretended not to hear. He said to Sophie, “You’re not to kill a single spider while I’m away.” And the door slammed behind him. Michael looked meaningly at Calcifer and sighed. Calcifer crackled with malicious laughter.

Since nobody explained where Howl had gone, Sophie concluded he was off to hunt young girls again and got down to work with more righteous vigor than ever. She did not dare harm any spiders after what Howl had said. So she banged at the beams with the broom, screaming, “Out, spiders! Out of my way!” Spiders scrambled for their lives every which way, and webs fell in swathes. Then of course she had to sweep the floor yet again. After that, she got down on her knees and scrubbed it.

“I wish you’d stop!” Michael said, sitting on the stairs out of her way.

Calcifer, cowering at the back of the grate, muttered, “I wish I’d never made that bargain with you now!”

Sophie scrubbed on vigorously. “You’ll be much happier when it’s all nice and clean,” she said.

“But I’m miserable now !” Michael protested.

Howl did not come back again until late that night. By that time Sophie had swept and scrubbed herself into a state when she could hardly move. She was sitting hunched up in the chair, aching all over. Michael took hold of Howl by a trailing sleeve and towed him over to the bathroom, where Sophie could hear him pouring out complaints in a passionate mutter. Phrases like “terrible old biddy” and “won’t listen to a word !” were quite easy to hear, even though Calcifer was roaring, “Howl, stop her! She’s killing us both!”

But all Howl said, when Michael let go of him, was “Did you kill any spiders?”

“Of course not!” Sophie snapped. Her aches made her irritable. “They look at me and run for their lives. What are they? All the girls whose hearts you ate?”

Howl laughed. “No, just simple spiders,” he said and went dreamily away upstairs.

Michael sighed. He went into the broom cupboard and hunted until he found an old folding bed, a straw mattress, and some rugs, which he put into the arched space under the stairs. “You’d better sleep here tonight,” he told Sophie.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Five: Which is far too full of washing

vocabulary.definition: Experiencing continuous mild pain.
vocabulary.example: She was sitting hunched up in the chair, aching all over.

vocabulary.definition: A natural liking or sympathy for something or someone.
vocabulary.example: Probably he had a wicked affinity with spiders, Sophie thought.

vocabulary.definition: A space shaped like an arch, often under stairs or ceilings.
vocabulary.example: ...rugs, which he put into the arched space under the stairs.

vocabulary.definition: A safe place to hide or escape to.
vocabulary.example: That leads to my private bolt hole and you are not being told where it is.

vocabulary.definition: A brush with a long handle used for sweeping floors.
vocabulary.example: Keep that broom still while I cross my own room, please.

vocabulary.definition: Crouching down in fear.
vocabulary.example: Calcifer, cowering at the back of the grate, muttered...

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows loss of hope.
vocabulary.example: When will you be back, Howl? Michael asked a little despairingly.

vocabulary.definition: Something or someone that brings shame or dishonour.
vocabulary.example: These cobwebs are a disgrace! Sophie declared...

vocabulary.definition: A fastening for a door that can be operated by a lever or knob.
vocabulary.example: As he put his hand on the door latch, she said...

vocabulary.definition: Decorative needlework on fabric.
vocabulary.example: Even the silver inlets and embroidery on his suit seemed to have become brighter.

vocabulary.definition: Bent over with the shoulders raised or forward.
vocabulary.example: She was sitting hunched up in the chair.

vocabulary.definition: A narrow opening or passage into a larger space.
vocabulary.example: Even the silver inlets and embroidery on his suit seemed to have become brighter.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows significance or intention without words.
vocabulary.example: Michael looked meaningly at Calcifer and sighed.

vocabulary.definition: Open, uncultivated hilly land, often with heather and peat.
vocabulary.example: He opened the door onto the wide, moving moorland and the hills.

vocabulary.definition: To speak indistinctly in a low tone.
vocabulary.example: He was pouring out complaints in a passionate mutter.

vocabulary.definition: Showing too much curiosity about other people's affairs.
vocabulary.example: What a nosy old woman you are! said Howl.

vocabulary.definition: Acted as if something were true when it was not.
vocabulary.example: Howl pretended not to hear.

vocabulary.definition: Morally right or justifiable.
vocabulary.example: ...got down to work with more righteous vigor than ever.

vocabulary.definition: Moved quickly and awkwardly.
vocabulary.example: Spiders scrambled for their lives every which way.

vocabulary.definition: To shut a door with force and noise.
vocabulary.example: And the door slammed behind him.

vocabulary.definition: Spoke sharply or abruptly due to irritation.
vocabulary.example: Of course not! Sophie snapped.

vocabulary.definition: Clean, smart, and well dressed.
vocabulary.example: He looked marvelously spruce.

vocabulary.definition: A large area or quantity of something.
vocabulary.example: Webs fell in swathes.

vocabulary.definition: A sleeve that hangs down loosely and drags behind.
vocabulary.example: Michael took hold of Howl by a trailing sleeve.

vocabulary.definition: Physical strength and good health; energy.
vocabulary.example: ...got down to work with more righteous vigor than ever.

vocabulary.definition: A gentle movement of air carrying a scent or sound.
vocabulary.example: Howl came out of the bathroom just then in a waft of steamy perfume.

vocabulary.definition: Evil or morally wrong; also slang for excellent or dangerous.
vocabulary.example: Probably he had a wicked affinity with spiders, Sophie thought.
